item.title: Mehdi Debates Right-Wing Podcasters on Iran, Israel and Immigration
item.description: If you’ve been waiting for a good-faith, wide-ranging, substantive, lengthy debate between Mehdi and the right on hot-button issues like Israel, Iran and immigration, well… the wait is over. It was two against one as Zeteo’s editor-in-chief appeared on the popular UK politics podcast…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
If you’ve been waiting for a good-faith, wide-ranging, substantive, lengthy debate between Mehdi and the right on hot-button issues like Israel, Iran and immigration, well… the wait is over.

It was two against one as Zeteo’s editor-in-chief appeared on the popular UK politics podcast ‘Triggernometry,’ to take on right-wing hosts Konstantin Kisin and Francis Foster.

Mehdi, as ever, didn’t hold back as the three of them discussed and debated:

How this Iran war began (“There’s a history of Israel bombing [Iran] every time we’re close to a deal”)

The role of AIPAC (“There is an Israel lobby that has massive, oversized influence on American politics”)

The left’s response to rising immigration (“You triggered me with ‘open borders’”)

Which Democratic candidates may have a chance of winning in 2028 (“Do I wish there was a more dynamic Democrat? Yes.”)

You can watch the full hour-plus debate above, which was filmed in Washington DC just over a week ago.

Do please support the important and independent journalism we do here at Zeteo by becoming a paid subscriber today, and/or by donating to Zeteo here.

If you are already a paid subscriber, first, thank you!, and second, do please feel free to give your feedback on the debate and especially on Mehdi’s performance in the comments below.

Don’t miss these other Zeteo stories:

item.title: The Campaign Against Hasan Piker Is About Crushing the Left
item.description: Donald Trump is leading a horrifying ethnic-cleansing campaign, gutting the Constitution, and trying to give himself king-like powers. He’s leading an illegal and reckless war against Iran, in partnership with Israel, that’s immiserating so many people here and abroad. Meanwhile, prominent…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Donald Trump is leading a horrifying ethnic-cleansing campaign, gutting the Constitution, and trying to give himself king-like powers. He’s leading an illegal and reckless war against Iran, in partnership with Israel, that’s immiserating so many people here and abroad.

Meanwhile, prominent Democrats – ranging from elected members of Congress, to 2026 Senate candidates, to likely 2028 presidential hopefuls – are spending their time condemning Hasan Piker, the left-wing streamer and influencer, as part of a cancellation campaign spearheaded by Third way, the centrist think tank.

Third Way, which is funded by billionaires and corporate interests, has been quite open about its broader goal and ongoing campaign to marginalize the left. “We will be the chief opponent of the left in the 2028 Democratic presidential primary,” the organization’s president, Jon Cowan, recently pledged. Asked to define the kind of lefty candidates Third Way doesn’t like, he pinpointed pols who “are for Medicare for All, the Green New Deal, abolishing ICE, open borders.”

Cowan told the New York Times that Third Way will “probably spend $30 million to $50 million” toward this campaign over the next few years.

In a memo last year, Third Way advised Democrats to build “a moderate Democratic infrastructure, including media, talent pipelines, and communications networks.” And it urged Dems to “reduce far-left influence and infrastructure,” neither of which meaningfully exists yet in US politics.

Third Way is targeting Piker as part of a broader effort to keep the Democratic Party under the control of big corporations, the ultra-wealthy, and supporters of Israel, no matter what the party’s base wants.

Piker doesn’t have as large an audience as Joe Rogan or Theo Von – the podcast bros whose audiences Donald Trump actively courted in 2024. He does, however, have millions of followers on Twitch, where he streams for hours each day, plus 1.6 million followers on Twitter. For a lefty influencer, Piker’s following is exceptional.

He is also getting involved politically. Last year, Piker interviewed Zohran Mamdani and loudly supported him. (Mamdani’s opponent, Andrew Cuomo, tried to make an issue out of it, and failed.)

On Saturday, Piker joined a livestream rally and fundraiser hosted by Progressive Victory to highlight several left-wing candidates, including Oliver Larkin, who’s running in Florida. Piker is set to campaign with Michigan Democratic Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed on April 7.

Now, Third Way is demanding Democrats keep Piker out of the party’s “big tent,” as its executives wrote in a recent column. They don’t want him, or anyone else, giving a boost to lefty candidates.

Some Democrats like that idea.

El-Sayed’s primary opponents, Haley Stevens (an AIPAC favorite) and Mallory McMorrow (backed by the moderate pro-Israel lobby J Street), have both slammed El-Sayed for rallying with Piker. McMorrow, who’s pitching herself as something of a middle-of-the-road progressive, went as far to compare Piker to neo-Nazi streamer Nick Fuentes.

While Piker has made his share of controversial remarks, the comparison is absurd.

“I’ve spent my entire professional media career, and far beyond that, combating antisemitism,” Piker said Saturday on the Progressive Victory stream. “I’ve been doxxed, swatted by neo-Nazis, for my consistent advocacy against antisemitism. I just also happen to be an avowed anti-Zionist.”

Illinois Democratic Rep. Brad Schneider, for his part, called Piker “an unapologetic antisemite,” imploring Democrats to keep him out of the party. “We cannot allow those who preach hate [and] seek division to find safe harbor among us,” Schneider wrote on Twitter.

Michigan Senator Elissa Slotkin criticized Piker, too, saying his rhetoric “sounds deeply antisemitic,” though she added: “I want to read for myself.”

Per a Politico report on Saturday, Slotkin joined at least two other potential 2028 contenders – Senators Cory Booker (D-N.J.) and Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) – in preemptively declaring that she would refuse to go on Piker’s stream.

“I find it really funny, because I never invited them onto my show,” Piker said Saturday.

Slotkin may not want to go on Piker’s show, but she still went on Bill Maher’s HBO show on Friday, despite his lengthy history of Islamophobic remarks.

On Saturday, the chairs of several county Democratic parties in New York issued a statement expressing “deep disappointment” with progressive House candidate Effie Phillips-Staley for joining Piker’s stream. She rejected their complaints.

The focus on Piker comes as Israel continues killing Palestinians with impunity, is attacking civilian infrastructure in Iran, and leads an invasion to seize territory in Lebanon. In recent days, a pro-Israel terrorist was caught plotting to kill a US citizen, Israel killed more journalists and medics, its soldiers violently assaulted a CNN photojournalist, and we learned that Israeli soldiers allegedly tortured a baby.

Third Way and Democratic politicians who want to police influencers’ anti-Israel rhetoric are signing up for a tough job.

“It is really remarkable that they’ve chosen a 90-to-10 issue in the Democratic Party’s base to draw a line in the sand over,” Piker said Saturday. “Most people understand that this is not about perceived antisemitism or anything like that. It’s absolutely about genocide. It’s absolutely about apartheid, and it’s absolutely about denying aid to Israel.”

Third Way may want to exercise control over who gets let into the Democratic Party, who advances in its primaries, and whose shows establishment Democrats decide to go on – and it sounds like the organization will have an enormous budget for its work of marginalizing the left.

The group can continue putting out columns that beg the left to stop using terms like “apartheid” and “genocide,” and demand that Democrats stop attacking the “Epstein class.”

Its execs can keep screaming about left-wing influencers and podcasters – and maybe, one day, Zeteo.

Ultimately, no one has to listen to what they say. We should take note of the politicians who do.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: This Week in Palestine: Israeli Forces Allegedly Torture Gaza Toddler to Force His Father to Confess
item.description: Israeli forces allegedly tortured a 1-year-old boy in Gaza to coerce a confession from his father – and the story barely got any media coverage at all. A leading UN expert said Israel is systematically torturing Palestinians. Most Western media didn’t seem to care. These are just two stories from…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Israeli forces allegedly tortured a 1-year-old boy in Gaza to coerce a confession from his father – and the story barely got any media coverage at all.

A leading UN expert said Israel is systematically torturing Palestinians. Most Western media didn’t seem to care.

These are just two stories from this week in Palestine – a week defined by torture – that likely wouldn’t have gotten much Western media attention even if the US wasn’t pursuing an illegal war, ICE wasn’t in US airports, and Donald Trump wasn’t spewing out his latest lies. But they’re stories, like the rest below, we at Zeteo didn’t miss, and are exactly why we launched this project a few weeks ago.

A quick note before we jump into this week: For now, we’re sending ‘This Week in Palestine’ to all Zeteo subscribers, but that won’t always be the case. To ensure you get the newsletter every week, go to ‘manage subscription’ in your Substack account, scroll down, and toggle on the button for ‘This Week in Palestine’. Please make sure you’re apaid Zeteo subscriber so you can read past the paywall.

Now, here’s another troubling ‘This Week in Palestine’:

Sunday, March 22 – Israel ‘Tortured’ 1-Year-Old Boy to Get Confession From Father, Family Says

Palestine journalist Osama Khalout reported that the family of 21-month-old Jawad Abu Nassar said they believe the toddler was tortured by Israeli forces to coerce a confession out of his father.

Israeli forces took the child after arresting his father, Osama, near the so-called yellow line near Gaza’s Maghazi refugee camp earlier this month. When Israel returned Jawad 10 hours later (a return Israeli forces filmed), his family noticed his pants were bloody, and he had marks on his legs.

“I observed multiple deep uniform lesions on his lower body, which are clinically consistent with deliberate cigarette burns used as a form of physical torture,” Dr. Bisan Ahmed, who conducted the examination of Jawad, told Sky News.

Israel denied the claims, suggesting to Sky News that the child may have suffered light injuries in the gunfire they claimed was directed towards the father and son in an attempt to get them to turn around. They also claimed Osama confessed to being a member of Hamas, and ridiculously said he was using his son as a “human shield.”

Osama remains in Israeli custody.

Monday, March 23 – World Has Given Israel ‘License to Torture Palestinians’

Israel’s systematic torture of Palestinians in Israeli prisons and beyond is “state doctrine,” UN Special Rapporteur Francesca Albanese said in a report.

“Israel has effectively been given a licence to torture Palestinians, because most of your governments, your ministers, have allowed it,” Albanese said, as she presented the report to the UN Human Rights Council. “Genocide has become the ultimate form of torture – continuous, generational, collective.”

Tuesday, March 24 – Judge Says Palestinian Teen Likely Starved in Israeli Prison, But Closes Case Anyways

An Israeli judge ruled that Waleed Ahmad, a 17-year-old Palestinian boy who died in Israeli prison last year, was likely starved, perHaaretz. But the judge closed the case, saying it wasn’t possible to establish a causal link between Ahmad’s poor condition and death.

Israel continues to hold Ahmad’s body.

Wednesday, March 25 – Israeli Lawmaker: No Innocent Palestinian Children

A far-right Israeli lawmaker said in the Knesset that there are “no innocent civilians or innocent children” in the West Bank city of Jenin.

Yitzahk Kroizer, a lawmaker with the extreme-right Otzma Yehudit party, was defending the Israeli soldiers who shot dead two Palestinian boys and their parents earlier this month as they were driving home in the occupied West Bank. The two other sons who survived the shooting were beaten by Israeli soldiers.

Thursday, March 26 – Israeli Settlers Kill Palestinian Man, Attack Others

Israeli settler violence continued all week, with at least three attacks just on Thursday. Israeli settlers killed Mohammad Ahmad Faraj, a Palestinian resident of East Jerusalem, and injured more than a dozen others in attacks across the occupied West Bank.

The attacks come as settlers erect new outposts on land in Area A of the West Bank, which is under the control of the Palestinian Authority, but is often raided by the Israeli military and, more recently, Israeli settlers.

Friday, March 27 – Israeli Forces Kill Palestinian at Funeral of Another Palestinian They Killed

Israeli forces killed 46-year-old Sufyan Ahmed Saleh Abu as he attended the funeral of 22-year-old Mustafa Asaad Mustafa Hamad, who was killed by the Israeli military earlier in the day in East Jerusalem’s Qalandia refugee camp, local media reported, citing the Palestinian Ministry of Health.

Several others were injured in both shootings.

Miss last week’s edition? Catch up here. Plus, check out more recent essays and interviews from Zeteo:

item.title: Our New World Order Is Here
item.description: This essay was first published by Arnaud Bertrand on his Substack. Zeteo is republishing it with his permission. By now, it’s painfully obvious that the Iran war is of a qualitatively different nature than most other US wars these past few decades. Take Vietnam, Afghanistan, Libya, Iraq, Serbia,…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
This essay was first published by Arnaud Bertrand on his Substack. Zeteo is republishing it with his permission.

By now, it’s painfully obvious that the Iran war is of a qualitatively different nature than most other US wars these past few decades.

Take Vietnam, Afghanistan, Libya, Iraq, Serbia, etc. (the list is unfortunately very long): the pattern was roughly always the same, with an immense power differential between aggressor and victim. These wars were, by and large, imperial: the empire attempting to crush a much weaker people whose only realistic recourse was guerrilla resistance. And that is when they actually had the will to resist: some – like Libya – barely even bothered, just resigning themselves to their fate.

As spectators of these wars, if you had any moral sense, the dominant emotion was a kind of helpless disgust: you were watching a giant stomp through someone else’s house.

Sure, the US actually lost many – if not most – of these wars, famously replacing the Taliban with the Taliban or being expelled with their tail between their legs from Vietnam, but the power differential was no less real for it. It’s just that power doesn’t always guarantee victory: sometimes the giant can’t kill everyone, and eventually tires of trying. But the “victories” won this way were always pyrrhic at best: the people endured, yes, but what they were left with was a country in ashes. Meanwhile, in the grand scheme of things, the giant walked away with little more than a bruised ego.

Iran is – remarkably – proving to be an entirely different beast: when others were merely surviving a giant, Iran appears to be able to compete with one.

Let’s look at the scoreboard as it stands today.

First of all, Iran is able, to this day, to retaliate – in kind, symmetrically and systematically – to US and Israeli attacks. Just this fact is absolutely extraordinary and unprecedented.

Take the recent US-Israeli strike on Iran’s Natanz Nuclear Facility. Iran had warned that it would not hesitate to target the Israeli nuclear site of Dimona – believed to host Israel’s atomic weapons program – in retaliation, which many dismissed as mere empty boasting, given that it is among the most protected strategic locations in the world, shielded by the full range of Israel’s and the US’s combined defense systems.

Well, it happened: on Saturday, Iran managed to strike the immediate vicinity of the nuclear site not once but twice, in two separate hits on both the town of Dimona and the town of Arad. And this isn’t Iranian propaganda, it’s been widely reported on by Israeli media and officially acknowledged by Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu himself.

Leaving the human toll aside, which is always regrettable (although it’s frankly hard to feel bad for Israel given the infinitely worse toll they’ve been inflicting on others), take a minute to reflect on how objectively remarkable this is and what it says about Iran’s real capabilities. No US adversary in living memory has been able to do anything like this: to announce a target weeks in advance – and not any target, one of the single most protected sites on the entire planet – face the full combined defensive arsenal of both the US and Israel, and still get through – twice.

We’re light-years away from a guerrilla fighter planting an IED trap by the roadside or the Vietcong laying booby traps in the jungle…

And this wasn’t a one-off. In fact, so far, virtually every time Iran has telegraphed it would hit a strategic target in retaliation for US-Israeli attacks, it’s successfully done so.

Another telling example is its strike on Israel’s Haifa refinery, which produces roughly half of Israel’s domestic fuel supplies, in retaliation for the Israeli attack on its South Pars gas facility. Again, the pattern is the same: a clearly telegraphed, symmetrical response – you strike our energy, we strike yours – executed against one of Israel’s most strategically vital and heavily defended sites.

Or take what is perhaps the most symbolically devastating example: on March 19, Iran’s air defenses struck a US F-35 during a combat mission over Iran, forcing it into an emergency landing – an incident confirmed by US Central Command. This is the first time in history that a fifth-generation stealth fighter has taken confirmed combat damage from enemy fire. The F-35 – at over $100 million a unit – is the crown jewel of American air power, the aircraft around which the entire US military doctrine of the future is built, and one long marketed as essentially unkillable by conventional air defenses.

To add insult to injury, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps released a video the same day showing the supposedly invisible and undetectable aircraft being tracked and struck, plainly visible on an Iranian targeting screen. According to multiple analysts, Iran likely used passive infrared sensors that detect engine heat rather than radar, which means the F-35’s entire design philosophy, its $1.7 trillion raison d’être as a radar-evading platform, was rendered irrelevant. Iran beat America’s stealth technology.

And this wasn’t a one-off: to date, as many as five F-15s may have been taken off the battlefield in this war. Three of them – we’re told by US Central Command – were lost to “friendly fire,” which would, quite the strange coincidence, make this one of the most accident-prone periods for the F-15 program in its entire history. All in all, according to confirmed US numbers, at least 20 US military aircraft have been destroyed since the start of the war, with a half-dozen other planes badly damaged in attacks or accidents.

And finally, perhaps the most ironic example of all: Iran destroyed at least one radar of a US THAAD missile defense battery stationed at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan – a fact confirmed by a US official and corroborated by satellite imagery published by CNN showing the charred remains of the system. THAAD – Terminal High Altitude Area Defense – is the very system designed to intercept and destroy incoming ballistic missiles. It is, quite literally, the shield. And Iran destroyed it with the very weapon it was built to stop. A $300 million radar system, the “eyes” of America’s most advanced missile defense architecture, taken out by the missiles it was supposed to see coming…

Worth mentioning, the US operates only eight THAAD batteries on the entire planet. Iran claims to have destroyed four radars used by them in a single 24-hour window – a claim that, if verified, would mean half the world’s supply of America’s most advanced missile defense sensors was wiped out in a day. What is verified is that the damage was severe enough to force the Pentagon to relocate parts of another THAAD system from South Korea to the Middle East - over Seoul’s explicit objections. In other words, to try to rebuild the shield Iran shattered in the Middle East, the US had to withdraw from Asia. Iran is not just challenging American power in one theater, it is forcing the redistribution of American power globally.

So that’s the first point: for the first time in decades, the United States, with Israel, are directly fighting an adversary that doesn’t just endure its blows, but returns them. Not through guerrilla attrition, not through roadside bombs and tunnel networks, but through direct, symmetrical, military retaliation against some of the most protected targets on the planet. You hit our nuclear facility, we hit yours. You strike our energy infrastructure, we strike yours. You think your most sophisticated weaponry protects you? Think again.

This makes the situation extremely – and perhaps unprecedentedly – dangerous. So dangerous in fact that we’re facing the very real possibility that large portions of the Middle East could become virtually uninhabitable. Something that even some Trump advisers, like the often prescient David Sacks, are openly acknowledging (his exact quote was “if you see that type of destruction continue, you could literally render the Gulf almost uninhabitable”).

I wish I was exaggerating, but that is genuinely where things stood as recently as a couple of days ago – and we might still be just one miscalculation away from it.

Donald Trump threatened on Saturday that the US would “hit and obliterate [Iran’s] various power plants, starting with the biggest one first” if Iran didn’t “fully open, without threat, the Strait of Hormuz within 48 hours from this exact point in time.”

Al Jazeera wrote that Iran’s response was that if the US did so, it’d “target US and Israel‑linked energy and IT infrastructure, along with desalination plants in the region.”

The desalination threat is exactly what could render the Gulf uninhabitable, as large swaths of the region are almost entirely reliant on desalination for their drinking water needs. Qatar derives 99% of its drinking water from desalination, Kuwait and Bahrain over 90%, Saudi Arabia 70%.

According to a 2008 memo from the US embassy in Saudi Arabia released by Wikileaks, if Iran were to hit Saudi Arabia’s Jubail desalination plant alone, “Riyadh would have to evacuate within a week.” That’s around 8 million people, one of the biggest cities in the world. The memo also noted that if the Jubail desalination plant was hit “the current structure of the Saudi government could not exist.”

So, there is the very real possibility we could be looking at the forced displacement of tens of millions of people and the collapse of multiple Gulf states, with one of the world’s most important regions – the very cradle of civilization – being rendered unlivable.

In any case, Iran’s threat seems to have landed. Before his 48-hour ultimatum was over, Trump posted that he had ordered the postponement of “ANY AND ALL MILITARY STRIKES AGAINST IRANIAN POWER PLANTS AND ENERGY INFRASTRUCTURE FOR A FIVE DAY PERIOD,” attributing his change of mind to “VERY GOOD AND PRODUCTIVE CONVERSATIONS” the United States had had with “THE COUNTRY OF IRAN, OVER THE LAST TWO DAYS.”

Iran immediately flatly denied that any conversation had taken place, with Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf posting on X that “no negotiations have been held with the US,” and Iranian embassies quite literally saying that Trump chickened out due to Iran’s threat to “target the energy infrastructure of the entire region.”

This means, remarkably, that Iran demonstrated in this instance that it had escalation dominance over the United States of America. That is, the ability to credibly threaten consequences so severe that the United States – for perhaps the first time since the Cold War – found it preferable to stand down.

Again, we’re light years away from your roadside IED or your jungle booby trap: Iran is turning out to be a genuinely powerful adversary, one capable of literally turning the Gulf into a wasteland if pushed far enough – and, crucially, credible enough that Washington believes it.

To be clear though, we’re anything but out of the woods. The war is not over, Trump’s new ultimatum is in 10 days (yes, he delayed it again), and there are apparently three separate 2,500-strong Marine expeditionary units converging on the Persian Gulf, with the Pentagon reportedly preparing detailed plans to seize Kharg Island. The pause on energy strikes may be less about “negotiations” than about buying time for this buildup.

Although, as Responsible Statecraft explains, the operation to take Kharg Island seems to be yet another neocon fever dream that would “land somewhere between a suicide mission and a self-imposed hostage crisis.” And, even if successful, would give the US little real leverage – which Iranian leader would surrender sovereignty in exchange for an oil terminal they already expected to be blown up? – and would do strictly nothing to reopen the Strait, which is more than 400 miles away. It’s not much more than a silver bullet fantasy, the likes of which neocons have been peddling for decades – always with the same promise that one more bold move will win the war, and pretty much always wrong.

You could also look at the economic dimension. Iran – and that was the reason behind Trump’s threat – is in control of the world’s most strategic energy chokepoint, the Strait of Hormuz. They’ve also managed to hit energy facilities in at least six countries, all supposedly defended by the US, all with major US military installations on their soil:

Israel - Haifa oil refinery, as we saw earlier

Qatar - Ras Laffan LNG terminal (an insane loss of revenue of $20B/year for Qatar)

Saudi Arabia - SAMREF refinery (Yanbu), Ras Tanura

UAE - Shah gas field, Habshan, Fujairah oil facility

Kuwait - Mina al-Ahmadi refinery, Mina Abdullah refinery

Bahrain - BAPCO oil refinery

This has provoked the most severe energy shock in modern history - worse than 1973, worse than 2022. One fifth of the world’s oil, a third of its LNG, a third of its fertilizer exports, and nearly half its sulphur – all of it effectively stopped. Destroyed infrastructure like Qatar’s Ras Laffan terminal – which on its own produces 20% of the world’s LNG – won’t be back online for years.

Oil prices have already surged over 60%. Countries across Asia are already rationing fuel. Sri Lanka has moved to a four-day work week. And that’s just the beginning: with 40% of the world’s fertilizer exports at risk right as planting season begins around the world, we are staring at a genuine global food crisis – not just higher prices (although that will very much happen too) but actual physical shortages and potential famines around the world.

As someone funnily (but bitterly) pointed on Twitter 👇

The stock market is indeed down (the S&P500 went down by as much as -5%, almost entirely due to the Iran war), but that should be the least of people’s worries right now…

Lastly, you could look at the political dimension – which may end up being the most consequential of all, because what’s unraveling isn’t just a military campaign, it’s the entire political architecture of American power in the Middle East.

Start with Iraq. Iran-aligned resistance factions have, through sustained drone and rocket attacks on US bases and the US Embassy in Baghdad, effectively forced the United States and NATO out of the country. NATO withdrew its entire mission – “several hundred” troops according to a NATO official (some sources speak of 600 personnel) – to Italy in what it euphemistically called a “temporary posture adjustment.” Romania evacuated all 117 of its soldiers. Poland, Spain, Italy – all pulled out. NATO C-130 aircraft reportedly couldn’t even land at the ironically-named Victory Base due to the intensity of the fire.

And then came the most extraordinary part: the US and NATO had to ask the Iraqi resistance factions – through the Iraqi government as intermediary – for a 24-hour ceasefire to allow them to complete their withdrawal safely. Let that sink in. The United States military – which invaded Iraq in 2003, toppled its government, and occupied the country for nearly two decades – had to ask permission from the people it once dismissed as insurgents to leave without being shot at on the way out.

This is arguably the most symbolically significant US military retreat since Afghanistan – and eerily similar.

Then look at the Gulf monarchies. For now, they appear to be sticking with the United States – but the precedent being set is devastating for America as a security guarantor. As we’ve just seen, Iran has struck energy facilities in six US-allied Gulf states despite every single one of them hosting major American military installations. The implicit political lesson writes itself: alignment with the United States doesn’t shield you; it exposes you.

Which is literally what some of the countries in the region are saying: Oman’s foreign minister – the very diplomat who mediated the nuclear talks between the US and Iran – published an extraordinary op-ed in theEconomist stating bluntly that Gulf states that “placed their trust in American security co-operation now experience that co-operation as an acute vulnerability.” You couldn’t make it clearer.

To be fair, not every actor in the region is drawing the same conclusion. UAE Foreign Minister Abdullah bin Zayed responded to the crisis by declaring, “we will never be blackmailed by terrorists” – this in reply to former French ambassador Gérard Araud, pointing out that the UAE’s dependence on the US had led it into a disastrous conflict without caring about its interests.

So there is a split between those already reading the writing on the wall and those doubling down on an alliance that just got their countries bombed. But even for those doubling down, the structural logic is unarguable: Iran has demonstrated that it can strike any Gulf state, and even potentially annihilate them completely, despite the full presence of American military infrastructure. That reality won’t be wished away by calling it blackmail.

Assuming Iran isn’t defeated in this war – and nothing so far suggests it will be – the Gulf monarchies will eventually have to choose between two security propositions. One where they stay aligned with a distant superpower that just proved that a) it not only can’t keep their refineries, their transportation hubs, or their drinking water safe, but b) it transforms all of these into targets. The other proposition being: make peace with the regional power that just proved it can hit all three whenever it wants. You can call this “blackmail” all you want, but indignation is not a security strategy.

The Gulf monarchies’ wavering is only one piece of a much larger political picture. Beyond the region, at a global level, the United States and Israel have never been so obviously isolated. Trump has publicly begged – there is no other word for it – China, France, Japan, South Korea, the UK – anyone! – to send warships to help reopen the Strait of Hormuz. The responses have essentially ranged from “f*ck you” to “f*ck you, but politely”. My favorite was Germany’s Defense Minister Boris Pistorius, who replied mockingly: “What does Trump expect a handful of European frigates to do that the powerful US Navy cannot? This is not our war, and we did not start it.”

China’s response was essentially, “are you kidding me right now? You started the war and you need to end it.” At least that’s the translation given by the Global Times, which called Trump’s ask an attempt to “share the risk of a war that Washington started and can’t finish.”

One can also appreciate the immense irony of Trump asking China for help policing the Strait of Hormuz when neocons around him – such as Lindsey Graham – said at the beginning of the war that it would prove to be “China’s nightmare.” The result, as Iran’s foreign minister sarcastically noted – rubbing salt in the wound – is that America is “now begging others, even China, to help it make Hormuz safe.”

In response, Trump – rather than reflecting on why the entire planet is refusing to help him – did what he does best: he lashed out. He called NATO a “paper tiger” and its members “COWARDS.” Which is, of course, self-defeating as it can only make them even less willing to help…

And it goes beyond government-level calculations. Across much of the world, the war has triggered genuine popular revolt. Even within the United States itself, an immense majority of Americans oppose the war. Only 33% support it according to YouGov, and 61% want it ended as quickly as possible. I couldn’t find any polls specifically on this, but I genuinely wouldn’t be surprised if more Americans are sympathizing with Iran in this fight than with their own government. I mean, look at this: 46% of Americans believe the US is responsible for the bombing of a girls’ school in Iran that killed 165 people – versus just 17% who say it isn’t. When nearly half your population thinks you’re killing innocent schoolgirls, “support the troops” starts to ring hollow.

There are also the political conditions that Iran has attached to passage in the Strait of Hormuz, which, contrary to US propaganda, is actually NOT closed, simply closed to them and other countries that help them attack Iran. As Foreign Minister Araghchi put it plainly: “We have not closed the strait. In our opinion, the strait is open. It is closed only to ships belonging to our enemies, countries that attack us. For other countries, ships can pass through the strait.”

And this distinction – little understood by most commentators – may in due course prove to be perhaps the single most consequential political move of the entire war.

In practice, the IRGC has established a controlled shipping corridor through Iranian territorial waters, threading between the islands of Larak and Qeshm – right past an IRGC naval base – where approved vessels are visually inspected as they pass. Ships wishing to transit must submit detailed ownership structures, cargo manifests, crew nationalities, and destination ports to the IRGC in advance. As one Kuwaiti consultancy put it bluntly: “This is not a reopening. It is a permission-based regime, where passage is granted selectively to ships linked to non-hostile or strategically aligned countries.”

Stop and think about what this means. India has negotiated passage. Pakistan has negotiated passage. Turkey has negotiated passage. Even France and Italy have reportedly opened talks with Tehran, according to none other than theFinancial Times. One by one, the nations of the world are going to Iran, cap in hand, to ask permission to use a waterway the United States had long claimed to police as the global guarantor of “freedom of navigation.”

And Iran is using this leverage not merely to extract transit fees but to advance its political vision. Araghchi told Al Jazeera that the first step “after the war” should be to “design new mechanisms for the Strait of Hormuz and the way ships pass to ensure permanent peaceful passage under specific regulations,” aligned with Iranian and regional interests. Iran’s parliament speaker Ghalibaf was even more explicit: “The Strait of Hormuz situation won’t return to its pre-war status.”

In effect, Iran is turning “freedom of navigation,” America’s single most invoked principle for projecting power across the world’s oceans, on its head: very concretely, it seems like the plan is for them to determine “freedom of navigation” in the Strait of Hormuz, with all the leverage that gives them.

More stunning still, Trump seems to have – at least partly – validated this plan, declaring on Monday, right after he backed down from his ultimatum, that he envisages the Strait of Hormuz being “jointly controlled by me and the Ayatollah.” To the amusement of Iran’s diplomacy 👇

Trump being Trump, this will probably mean nothing tomorrow. But even as empty rhetoric, it represents an extraordinary concession: the United States publicly floating shared control of the world’s most strategic waterway with the very country Trump is at war with, which tells you how far the ground has shifted.

It’s not just Trump: every country that picks up the phone to negotiate passage with Tehran is, whether it admits it or not, implicitly recognizing the new reality that Iran is a sovereign actor with legitimate security interests in its own neighborhood, and that those interests must be accommodated rather than crushed. Every tanker that sails through the Larak-Qeshm corridor with IRGC approval is a small, quiet vote for a post-American order in the Middle East.

And the most remarkable part? The United States obviously has no answer for this. Even begging others, including adversaries, for help – something which, given Trump’s ego, must have taken a big toll – hasn’t worked.

So let’s recap. Militarily, Iran has done what no US adversary has managed in decades: not merely survive American firepower, but match it – striking the most protected targets on the planet, damaging America’s most advanced aircraft, and destroying the very missile defense systems designed to stop it. Economically, it has triggered the worst energy shock in modern history and taken control of the world’s most strategic chokepoint – all while the US watches, unable to reopen the Strait even with its full naval might deployed. Politically, it has forced the US out of Iraq, exposed the hollow promise of American security guarantees to its supposed “allies,” achieved escalation dominance over Washington itself, and – perhaps most consequentially – established a permission-based transit regime through the Strait of Hormuz that is quietly writing the rules of the post-American Middle East, one negotiated passage at a time. The United States, meanwhile, is isolated internationally, opposed domestically, and reduced to begging adversaries for help that isn’t coming.

All of that in 3 weeks.

This article isn’t meant to be a celebration of Iran, but an honest reckoning with what we’re witnessing. Call it what it is: the first genuine multipolar war. Historians may well look back at this conflict the way they look back at Suez in 1956 – not as a military event as much as a revelation: the moment the reality of a new order became undeniable.

For what is multipolarity, after all? It is, by definition, the existence of poles of power that cannot be submitted by other poles. We already knew Russia and China were poles, and that’s precisely why the US hasn’t gone to war against them – and likely never will – but Iran was filed under a different category: a small regional power that could be crushed if it ever came to it.

What this war is revealing is that this categorization was wrong. Iran can, in fact, hold its own against the full weight of American military power.

We don’t know yet how, or when, this war will end. Iran may still lose. What we do know for a fact is that this war has already established several precedents, each with immense implications:

Even a non-nuclear middle power can achieve some form of escalation dominance over the United States. You don’t necessarily need to have nukes, just the ability to credibly threaten sufficiently severe consequences.

America’s most advanced weapons systems are much more vulnerable than previously thought – not theoretically, but in actual combat.

The US security guarantee has been empirically falsified in real time. Six countries hosting American military bases had their critical infrastructure struck.

A single regional power can hold the global economy hostage through control of a strategic chokepoint, and no combination of forces has been able to break that control.

The US president has publicly floated being open to jointly controlling the Strait of Hormuz with Iran, an admittedly extraordinary admission.

Think about what those precedents imply if you’re, say, Turkey, or Brazil, or Indonesia, or any of the dozens of mid-sized powers that were told their only options were compliance or annihilation. The menu just got a lot longer.

Think about what it says if you’re Saudi Arabia, quietly watching your American-built defenses fail to protect your own refineries. Or any European country now facing the worst energy shock since 1973, caused not by your enemy but by your ally, and realizing that said “ally,” supposedly in charge of “protecting” you, couldn’t even protect Israel’s most strategic sites – when it’s the country with which it’s joined at the hip.

I’m not even speaking about China or Russia, which are seeing their worldview being validated on almost every axis simultaneously. American military supremacy being overstated? Check. US security guarantees aren’t worth the paper they’re written on? Check. The Western alliance system is now largely a fiction? Check (even Trump called NATO “a paper tiger”!). Sanctions don’t work? Check. You cannot build your security at the expense of others’? Check (six countries just learned that the hard way). That pouring trillions into green energy was strategic foresight, not “overcapacity”? Check – China is one of the least exposed major economies to the worst energy shock in modern history. And, of course, that the US is the aggressor, the primary source of instability in the world? Check – it started this war unprovoked.

There is almost a Greek tragedy quality to US actions lately where every move taken to escape one’s fate becomes the mechanism that delivers it. The US went to war to reassert dominance – and proved it could no longer dominate. It demanded allies send warships – and revealed it had no real allies. It waged 40 years of maximum pressure to break Iran before this moment came – and instead forged the very adversary now capable of meeting it. It started the war in part to have additional leverage over China – and handed the world the spectacle of begging China for help. The prophecy was multipolarity. Every American action to prevent it reveals it instead.

Arnaud Bertrand is an entrepreneur who founded HouseTrip (acquired by TripAdvisor) and now Me & Qi. Arnaud writes about China, where he lived for eight years, as well as entrepreneurship and geopolitics. Subscribe to his Substack for more of his writing.

The views expressed in this article are the author’s own and do not necessarily reflect those of Zeteo.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: Has Trump Started Something He Can’t End? Tommy Vietor on Iran, Iraq, and the Democrats
item.description: Can Donald Trump really end this war on Iran? Where does the US go from there? Ex-Obama NSC aide and Pod Save America co-host Tommy Vietor joins Zeteo’s John Harwood on Substack Live to break down Trump’s shambolic war, the president’s bizarre mental state, whether this nightmare of an…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Can Donald Trump really end this war on Iran? Where does the US go from there?

Ex-Obama NSC aide and Pod Save America co-host Tommy Vietor joins Zeteo’s John Harwood on Substack Live to break down Trump’s shambolic war, the president’s bizarre mental state, whether this nightmare of an administration is largely a consequence of having elected a Black president in 2008, and much more.

Watch the full video above to hear Vietor and Harwood compare the Iran and Iraq wars, discuss whether ‘No Kings’ will make a difference, and dissect how Democrats will fare in the midterms – and whether Trump could actually stop the elections!

Free subscribers can watch a 5-minute preview. Consider upgrading to a paid subscription to never hit a paywall again!

This was a live conversation on Zeteo.com and the Substack app, where subscribers could ask questions. Be sure to subscribe to Zeteo to catch the next Substack Live.

Catch up on more from Zeteo:

item.title: About that ‘Gay Ayatollah’ Story...
item.description: 🚨 Breaking: Early this morning, the Senate passed legislation that would fund most of the Department of Homeland Security, including TSA, but not ICE and Border Patrol. The bill now faces an uncertain future in the House. Good morning! Swin and Andrew here, with a story of war, intrigue, and…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
🚨 Breaking: Early this morning, the Senate passed legislation that would fund most of the Department of Homeland Security, including TSA, but not ICE and Border Patrol. The bill now faces an uncertain future in the House.

Good morning! Swin and Andrew here, with a story of war, intrigue, and psychological warfare in the Trump-Vance era, one that “a lot of people” are talking about, as Donald Trump likes to say.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we peel back the onion on the “gayatollah” story put out about Mojtaba Khamenei, Trump will be the first president to put his signature on US dollars, and does it feel like the US president always escalates his war on the weekends?

Inside the ‘Gayatollah’ MAGA Group Chat

In recent weeks, following the killing of Iran’s Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, several Trump administration officials and some of their close outside allies cooked up a plan to run a psyop against their Iranian enemies and the new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, four sources tell us.

The idea was to plant a public narrative about how US intelligence agencies had classified information strongly indicating that Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei is gay and closeted. Under the regime in Tehran, LGBTQ Iranians are, of course, persecuted – and during Trump’s war on Iran, his administration has sought to sow discord, chaos, and division among the Iranian people and their government.

There is one issue, though: There is no intel, certainly no credible intel, indicating he’s gay – the idea was fabricated and pushed behind the scenes in the past several weeks by a cohort of Trump advisers, US government personnel, and MAGAworld associates, two people with knowledge of the matter and another two sources briefed on it tell us.

Some of them have been having a good laugh about it ever since. “We wanted to mindfuck [the Iranians] with gay shit,” one of the knowledgeable sources says. Another source – a Trump administration official familiar with how this all went down – adds that one of their MAGA group chats is titled, simply: “gayatollah.”

By late Thursday night, the White House had not responded to our request for comment. However, not long after we emailed our comment request, Trump suddenly appeared on TV blabbing about the topic.

The president was asked by Fox’s Jesse Watters if the CIA had told him that the new supreme leader is gay. “Well, they did say that, but I don’t know if it was only them,” Trump replied. “I think a lot of people are saying that, which puts him off to a bad start in that particular country.”

As you would expect, Trump went on to claim he “did very well with the gay vote” (he did not) and that “no Republican’s ever gotten the gay vote like I did.”

The “gayatollah” tale burst into the public domain earlier this month – in spectacular, tabloid-y fashion.

The New York Post, one of President Trump’s favorite big-city tabloid newspapers, ran an “EXCLUSIVE” headlined: “Trump briefed that Iran’s new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei is probably gay – and president has priceless reaction.” The reporting was sourced to “two intelligence community officials and a third person close to the White House.”

The story went viral, and got some play in the international press. ‘The Daily Show’ did a segment riffing on it. The Post reported that “Trump couldn’t contain his surprise and laughed aloud when he was briefed on the intel, according to sources,” though when he was asked about the story on camera last week, the president did not seem too interested and just rambled about other topics.

As word of the alleged intel on the “probably gay” Iranian leader spread, various current and former US officials who weren’t directly involved with this psyop immediately viewed it as an obvious ratfuck from Team Trump or its allies. Several tell us that they see no indication that the Iranians are buying it.

A fairly large number of our sources – who each had regular, senior-level visibility into secret info on Iran and its elites while working in the US government under Presidents Obama, Trump 1.0, Biden, or Trump 2.0 – all say they are unaware of any US intelligence about the younger Khamenei and a secret gay identity. Several said some version of: If this were a thing, I would have known about it.

“No one [here] gives it any credit; I doubt anybody in the Middle East does either,” says one current US official. “It’s some 20-year-old frat boy in the federal government’s idea of a good joke.”

It is true that this latest, weird mini-saga in Trump’s illegal war against Iran can be thought of as a MAGA-frat-kid version of how the CIA apparently commissioned a pornographic movie titled ‘Happy Days’ starring a fake Sukarno, as the agency sought to wreck the then Indonesian leader’s reputation among his people during the Cold War.

The Iran-war-related psyop is also vaguely reminiscent of the psychological warfare that the 2024 Trump campaign repeatedly deployed against Florida’s GOP governor and Trump’s then-nemesis, Ron DeSantis. It got to the point that the Trump campaign kept pushing gross, unverified gossip about DeSantis’s bowel movements.

Donald Trump’s Iran war: horrifying, bloody, and also extremely childish and stupid. Don’t expect that to change anytime soon.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s deputy health minister told Al Jazeera today that at least 1,937 people had been killed in US-Israeli attacks. Iranian officials said earlier this month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured.

Lebanon: Israel has killed at least 1,116 people, including more than 120 children, in Lebanon, according to yesterday’sfigures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 19, including three Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks in Israel.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Delayed war crime: Trump announced that he is again postponing his deadline for a planned ‘destruction’ of Iran’s energy infrastructure in an effort to get the country to open up the Strait of Hormuz.

‘Final blow’: The Pentagon is weighing several plans for ground invasions in Iran; options include seizing Kharg Island, Larak Island, or the Abu Musa islands, or seizing ships exporting Iranian oil, Axios reported.

More boots: Trump has already moved thousands of troops to the Middle East for a potential ground invasion – and his Pentagon could now send 10,000 more.

Dems bailing: House Democrats may have had the votes for a War Powers resolution to stop Trump’s war in Iran before the congressional recess, but Dem Rep. Gregory Meeks didn’t hold the vote – even as GOP Rep. Nancy Mace signaled her support for it to Prem. (Watch here.)

Annexation fears: Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam told UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres he’s concerned about Israel annexing southern Lebanon, given its mass displacement of civilians and destruction of homes. Deputy PM Tarek Mitri sounded the alarm about Israel committing war crimes.

Desperate for weapons: As the US Defense Department becomes strapped for cash, having spent roughly $25 billion on the war so far and asked for $200 billion more from Congress, the Pentagon is looking to re-route weapons designated for Ukraine.

Waiting for the Weekend

As Trump and his party bumble about their bloodletting in the Middle East, we at Zeteo have recently heard from sources close to this president about something he’s mentioned a number of times to close advisers: When it comes to major attacks on Iran, Trump has privately said “on weekends is better,” or pointedly asked about if certain strikes or US military actions would occur over the weekend.

This has been interpreted by several senior US officials as the president telling them that he’d prefer their big attacks to commence after the markets close for the weekend.

In response to our reporting, White House spokesman Kush Desai said in a statement:

“Pinpointing when and where exactly the entire Iranian terror regime’s secretive leadership would be gathering, as well as knowing when and where Nicolas Maduro would be most vulnerable to be extracted flawlessly without any U.S. casualties or equipment loss, required immense intelligence gathering and operational planning. The idea that the timing of these military operations was predicated on when the stock markets were closed as opposed to what the intelligence suggested and what was operationally optimal for mission success is so hairbrained that only someone who has never even planned a toddler’s birthday party could believe it.”

We want to see the evidence that Donald J. Trump has planned a birthday party for any of his kids. (Swin, one of the co-authors of today’s newsletter, has helped plan several, for what it’s worth.)

🗞️ What You Need to Know

TSA pay: Just hours before the Senate passed its legislation, as Republicans refused to pass a bill to pay TSA workers, Trump suddenly announced he was ordering DHS to pay them, “to quickly stop the Democrat Chaos at the Airports.” DHS remains partially shut down over Democrats’ demand for ICE reforms.

Poor Pete: Donald Trump dissed his defense secretary, saying someone told him he “made a mistake” picking Pete Hegseth. Finding suspects may prove tricky. Per Zeteo’s reporting, Hegseth’s Pentagon nickname is “Dumb McNamara.”

Cult cashes in: Not content with plastering his face over Washington and smashing up the White House, Trump plans to add his signature to all denominations of US bills, Vanity Fair reported. No president has ever affixed his signature to money.

Data deal: CBS reported that the Departments of Justice and Homeland Security want to allow the use of voter registration data in criminal and immigration probes. The aim is to stop non-immigrant voting, which experts agree doesn’t exist.

AI ruling: A California judge temporarily blocked the Pentagon from blacklisting AI firm Anthropic over a disagreement about the use of its technology in warfare. Judge Rita F. Lin said the Trump administration acted in “Orwellian” fashion.

Platner boost: As Maine Governor Janet Mills released another ad about Graham Platner’s comments about sexual assault, the oysterman surged in a Democratic Senate primary poll taken after the first such attack. It put Platner 27 points up!

🧠 Pop Quiz!

Don’t tell Donald Trump, but one president managed to get his face on a $100,000 dollar bill, the largest denomination ever printed. Which one?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: The director general of the ridiculously named “Board of Peace” introduced a plan that would entail a gradual disarmament of Hamas and other Palestinian groups – an Israeli demand Hamas has flatly rejected while the occupation continues – and in return supposedly allows reconstruction materials and increased aid to enter Gaza.

🇾🇪🇩🇯 Lost at sea: At least nine migrants died, and 45 were missing after a shipwreck off Djibouti while traveling from the East African country to Yemen via the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, the International Organization for Migration said.

🇳🇱 Musk trouble: A Dutch court ordered xAI’s Grok to stop generating sexual imagery of individuals without consent, with a fine of €100,000 (around $115,000) a day for noncompliance, holding Elon Musk accountable for a lack of control of the tool.

🇭🇺 Spy claim: Officials in Hungary’s Putin-aligned government announced an investigation of journalist Szabolcs Panyi, who has worked to uncover government ties to Russia, claiming he is spying for Ukraine.

Fox Watch

Only Donald Trump can answer a serious question from a Fox host about the condition of the Iranian people by telling that host… how good-looking she is (!)

🧠 Trivia answer: Woodrow Wilson, on a bill printed in 1934 for internal Federal Reserve use.

📖 Weekend Reads

CNN’s “Podcast Look” and the Slow Death of Cable News

Why is CNN having Jake Tapper and Anderson Cooper morph their broadcast shows into a podcast-style setup that is bound to fail? In this New Yorker article, Jay Caspian Kang explains what CNN’s hopeless attempt at connecting with viewers says about the state of cable news.

The organizing model that failed the Democrats

Despite knocking on more doors and making more phone calls than ever before, Democrats still lost the 2024 election – badly. While there are a number of reasons for the failure, in this Chaotic Era piece, Greta Carnes and Kyle Tharp explore why an outdated organizing model deserves its share of the blame.

Heroism, horror, and the ‘pits of hell’: inside the last days of El Fasher

Over two days in October 2025, up to 10,000 people were massacred in El Fasher, the capital of Sudan’s North Darfur state, with 40,000 unaccounted for. As Sudan’s civil war rages on, The Guardian investigates why “the most explicitly anticipated mass atrocity event ever” happened, and breaks down the sheer brutality of the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF).

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly, Akshay Gokul, and Melanie Riehl contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

item.title: About that ‘Gay Ayatollah’ Story...

The bill now faces an uncertain future in the House. Good morning! Swin and Andrew here, with a story of war, intrigue, and…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
🚨 Breaking: Early this morning, the Senate passed legislation that would fund most of the Department of Homeland Security, including TSA, but not ICE and Border Patrol. The bill now faces an uncertain future in the House.

Good morning! Swin and Andrew here, with a story of war, intrigue, and psychological warfare in the Trump-Vance era, one that “a lot of people” are talking about, as Donald Trump likes to say.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we peel back the onion on the “gayatollah” story put out about Mojtaba Khamenei, Trump will be the first president to put his signature on US dollars, and does it feel like the US president always escalates his war on the weekends?

Inside the ‘Gayatollah’ MAGA Group Chat

In recent weeks, following the killing of Iran’s Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, several Trump administration officials and some of their close outside allies cooked up a plan to run a psyop against their Iranian enemies and the new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, four sources tell us.

The idea was to plant a public narrative about how US intelligence agencies had classified information strongly indicating that Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei is gay and closeted. Under the regime in Tehran, LGBTQ Iranians are, of course, persecuted – and during Trump’s war on Iran, his administration has sought to sow discord, chaos, and division among the Iranian people and their government.

There is one issue, though: There is no intel, certainly no credible intel, indicating he’s gay – the idea was fabricated and pushed behind the scenes in the past several weeks by a cohort of Trump advisers, US government personnel, and MAGAworld associates, two people with knowledge of the matter and another two sources briefed on it tell us.

Some of them have been having a good laugh about it ever since. “We wanted to mindfuck [the Iranians] with gay shit,” one of the knowledgeable sources says. Another source – a Trump administration official familiar with how this all went down – adds that one of their MAGA group chats is titled, simply: “gayatollah.”

By late Thursday night, the White House had not responded to our request for comment. However, not long after we emailed our comment request, Trump suddenly appeared on TV blabbing about the topic.

The president was asked by Fox’s Jesse Watters if the CIA had told him that the new supreme leader is gay. “Well, they did say that, but I don’t know if it was only them,” Trump replied. “I think a lot of people are saying that, which puts him off to a bad start in that particular country.”

As you would expect, Trump went on to claim he “did very well with the gay vote” (he did not) and that “no Republican’s ever gotten the gay vote like I did.”

The “gayatollah” tale burst into the public domain earlier this month – in spectacular, tabloid-y fashion.

The New York Post, one of President Trump’s favorite big-city tabloid newspapers, ran an “EXCLUSIVE” headlined: “Trump briefed that Iran’s new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei is probably gay – and president has priceless reaction.” The reporting was sourced to “two intelligence community officials and a third person close to the White House.”

The story went viral, and got some play in the international press. ‘The Daily Show’ did a segment riffing on it. The Post reported that “Trump couldn’t contain his surprise and laughed aloud when he was briefed on the intel, according to sources,” though when he was asked about the story on camera last week, the president did not seem too interested and just rambled about other topics.

As word of the alleged intel on the “probably gay” Iranian leader spread, various current and former US officials who weren’t directly involved with this psyop immediately viewed it as an obvious ratfuck from Team Trump or its allies. Several tell us that they see no indication that the Iranians are buying it.

A fairly large number of our sources – who each had regular, senior-level visibility into secret info on Iran and its elites while working in the US government under Presidents Obama, Trump 1.0, Biden, or Trump 2.0 – all say they are unaware of any US intelligence about the younger Khamenei and a secret gay identity. Several said some version of: If this were a thing, I would have known about it.

“No one [here] gives it any credit; I doubt anybody in the Middle East does either,” says one current US official. “It’s some 20-year-old frat boy in the federal government’s idea of a good joke.”

It is true that this latest, weird mini-saga in Trump’s illegal war against Iran can be thought of as a MAGA-frat-kid version of how the CIA apparently commissioned a pornographic movie titled ‘Happy Days’ starring a fake Sukarno, as the agency sought to wreck the then Indonesian leader’s reputation among his people during the Cold War.

The Iran-war-related psyop is also vaguely reminiscent of the psychological warfare that the 2024 Trump campaign repeatedly deployed against Florida’s GOP governor and Trump’s then-nemesis, Ron DeSantis. It got to the point that the Trump campaign kept pushing gross, unverified gossip about DeSantis’s bowel movements.

Donald Trump’s Iran war: horrifying, bloody, and also extremely childish and stupid. Don’t expect that to change anytime soon.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s deputy health minister told Al Jazeera today that at least 1,937 people had been killed in US-Israeli attacks. Iranian officials said earlier this month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured.

Lebanon: Israel has killed at least 1,116 people, including more than 120 children, in Lebanon, according to yesterday’sfigures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 19, including three Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks in Israel.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Delayed war crime: Trump announced that he is again postponing his deadline for a planned ‘destruction’ of Iran’s energy infrastructure in an effort to get the country to open up the Strait of Hormuz.

‘Final blow’: The Pentagon is weighing several plans for ground invasions in Iran; options include seizing Kharg Island, Larak Island, or the Abu Musa islands, or seizing ships exporting Iranian oil, Axios reported.

More boots: Trump has already moved thousands of troops to the Middle East for a potential ground invasion – and his Pentagon could now send 10,000 more.

Dems bailing: House Democrats may have had the votes for a War Powers resolution to stop Trump’s war in Iran before the congressional recess, but Dem Rep. Gregory Meeks didn’t hold the vote – even as GOP Rep. Nancy Mace signaled her support for it to Prem. (Watch here.)

Annexation fears: Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam told UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres he’s concerned about Israel annexing southern Lebanon, given its mass displacement of civilians and destruction of homes. Deputy PM Tarek Mitri sounded the alarm about Israel committing war crimes.

Desperate for weapons: As the US Defense Department becomes strapped for cash, having spent roughly $25 billion on the war so far and asked for $200 billion more from Congress, the Pentagon is looking to re-route weapons designated for Ukraine.

Waiting for the Weekend

As Trump and his party bumble about their bloodletting in the Middle East, we at Zeteo have recently heard from sources close to this president about something he’s mentioned a number of times to close advisers: When it comes to major attacks on Iran, Trump has privately said “on weekends is better,” or pointedly asked about if certain strikes or US military actions would occur over the weekend.

This has been interpreted by several senior US officials as the president telling them that he’d prefer their big attacks to commence after the markets close for the weekend.

“Pinpointing when and where exactly the entire Iranian terror regime’s secretive leadership would be gathering, as well as knowing when and where Nicolas Maduro would be most vulnerable to be extracted flawlessly without any U.S. casualties or equipment loss, required immense intelligence gathering and operational planning. We want to see the evidence that Donald J. Trump has planned a birthday party for any of his kids. (Swin, one of the co-authors of today’s newsletter, has helped plan several, for what it’s worth.)

🗞️ What You Need to Know

Poor Pete: Donald Trump dissed his defense secretary, saying someone told him he “made a mistake” picking Pete Hegseth. Finding suspects may prove tricky. Per Zeteo’s reporting, Hegseth’s Pentagon nickname is “Dumb McNamara.”

Cult cashes in: Not content with plastering his face over Washington and smashing up the White House, Trump plans to add his signature to all denominations of US bills, Vanity Fair reported. No president has ever affixed his signature to money.

Data deal: CBS reported that the Departments of Justice and Homeland Security want to allow the use of voter registration data in criminal and immigration probes. The aim is to stop non-immigrant voting, which experts agree doesn’t exist.

AI ruling: A California judge temporarily blocked the Pentagon from blacklisting AI firm Anthropic over a disagreement about the use of its technology in warfare. Judge Rita F. Lin said the Trump administration acted in “Orwellian” fashion.

Platner boost: As Maine Governor Janet Mills released another ad about Graham Platner’s comments about sexual assault, the oysterman surged in a Democratic Senate primary poll taken after the first such attack. It put Platner 27 points up!

🧠 Pop Quiz!

Don’t tell Donald Trump, but one president managed to get his face on a $100,000 dollar bill, the largest denomination ever printed. Which one?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: The director general of the ridiculously named “Board of Peace” introduced a plan that would entail a gradual disarmament of Hamas and other Palestinian groups – an Israeli demand Hamas has flatly rejected while the occupation continues – and in return supposedly allows reconstruction materials and increased aid to enter Gaza.

🇾🇪🇩🇯 Lost at sea: At least nine migrants died, and 45 were missing after a shipwreck off Djibouti while traveling from the East African country to Yemen via the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, the International Organization for Migration said.

🇳🇱 Musk trouble: A Dutch court ordered xAI’s Grok to stop generating sexual imagery of individuals without consent, with a fine of €100,000 (around $115,000) a day for noncompliance, holding Elon Musk accountable for a lack of control of the tool.

🇭🇺 Spy claim: Officials in Hungary’s Putin-aligned government announced an investigation of journalist Szabolcs Panyi, who has worked to uncover government ties to Russia, claiming he is spying for Ukraine.

Fox Watch

Only Donald Trump can answer a serious question from a Fox host about the condition of the Iranian people by telling that host… how good-looking she is (!)

🧠 Trivia answer: Woodrow Wilson, on a bill printed in 1934 for internal Federal Reserve use.

📖 Weekend Reads

CNN’s “Podcast Look” and the Slow Death of Cable News

Why is CNN having Jake Tapper and Anderson Cooper morph their broadcast shows into a podcast-style setup that is bound to fail? In this New Yorker article, Jay Caspian Kang explains what CNN’s hopeless attempt at connecting with viewers says about the state of cable news.

The organizing model that failed the Democrats

Despite knocking on more doors and making more phone calls than ever before, Democrats still lost the 2024 election – badly. While there are a number of reasons for the failure, in this Chaotic Era piece, Greta Carnes and Kyle Tharp explore why an outdated organizing model deserves its share of the blame.

Over two days in October 2025, up to 10,000 people were massacred in El Fasher, the capital of Sudan’s North Darfur state, with 40,000 unaccounted for.
